title: Generic type parameters should be co/contravariant when possible
description: In the interests of making code as usable as possible, interfaces and delegates with generic parameters should use the out and in modifiers when possible to make the interfaces and delegates covariant and contravariant, respectively. The out keyword can be used when the type parameter is used only as a return type in the interface or delegate. Doing so makes the parameter covariant, and allows interface and delegate instances created with a sub-type to be used as instances created with a base type.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant: public interface IHandler<T> // Noncompliant: could be contravariant
compliant: public interface IHandler<in T> // Compliant: contravariant
public interface IProvider<T> // Noncompliant: could be covariant
public interface IProvider<out T> // Compliant: covariant

logic.detection_conditions: Detects generic interfaces/delegates that could use variance modifiers (in/out) but don't
logic.triggering_conditions: Triggers when: generic interface or delegate declaration lacks in/out variance modifiers where they might be applicable
logic.exclusion_conditions: Excludes: interfaces/delegates already using in/out, non-generic types, comments
never_trigger_on: Classes (only interfaces and delegates support variance)
logic.reasoning: This rule requires deep semantic analysis to determine if variance modifiers are applicable. Proper detection needs to: 1) Identify generic interfaces/delegates, 2) Analyze type parameter usage (input vs output), 3) Determine if 'in' (contravariant) or 'out' (covariant) is safe. Without semantic analysis, we can only detect generic interface/delegate declarations that lack variance modifiers as candidates for manual review. A heuristic approach: detect 'interface' or 'delegate' with generic parameter '<T>' not already marked 'in T' or 'out T'. Full validation requires type flow analysis.

should_trigger: public interface IHandler<T> { void Handle(T item); }
should_not_trigger: public interface IHandler<in T> { void Handle(T item); }
public interface IProvider<out T> { T GetItem(); }
